name: config
context: Next.js configuration reference — next.config.js/mjs options for images, redirects, rewrites, headers, webpack, and output modes
info: The `next.config.js` (or `next.config.mjs` / `next.config.ts`) file at the project root configures Next.js build and runtime behavior. Key configuration areas include: image optimization domains and loaders, URL redirects and rewrites, custom response headers, environment variables, webpack customization, Turbopack settings, output modes (standalone for Docker, export for static HTML), base path and trailing slash settings, TypeScript and ESLint configuration, React Strict Mode, compiler options (SWC transforms), and experimental features. The config file is evaluated at build time in a Node.js environment. Agents frequently modify this file when adding image domains, setting up redirects, enabling experimental features, or configuring deployment targets.

sections.title: Image optimization
sections.body: Configure `images.remotePatterns` to allow external image domains for the `<Image>` component. Each pattern specifies `protocol`, `hostname` (supports wildcards with `**`), `port`, and `pathname`. For custom CDN loaders (Cloudinary, Imgix), set `images.loader: 'custom'` and `images.loaderFile`. Configure `deviceSizes` and `imageSizes` for responsive breakpoints. For static export, set `images.unoptimized: true`.

sections.title: Redirects and rewrites
sections.body: Define `redirects()` and `rewrites()` as async functions returning arrays of rules. Redirects change the browser URL (`permanent: true` for 308, `permanent: false` for 307). Rewrites serve content from a different path without changing the URL — useful for proxying external APIs. Both support `:param` path matching and `has` conditions for headers, cookies, and query parameters. Redirects and rewrites are evaluated at request time.

sections.title: Output modes
sections.body: `output: 'standalone'` creates a self-contained build with all dependencies included — ideal for Docker deployments. The output goes to `.next/standalone/` with a minimal `server.js`. `output: 'export'` generates static HTML files in `out/` — no Node.js server needed, deployable to any static host. Static export has limitations: no Server Components, no API routes, no middleware, no ISR. See [[nextjs/deployment]] for deployment guides.

sections.title: Environment variables
sections.body: Next.js loads `.env.local`, `.env.development`, `.env.production`, and `.env` files automatically. Variables prefixed with `NEXT_PUBLIC_` are inlined at build time and exposed to the browser. Server-only variables (no prefix) are available in Server Components, API routes, middleware, and `getServerSideProps`. Use `.env.local` for secrets (git-ignored by default). Build-time env vars via `next.config.js` `env` option are an alternative but `.env` files are preferred.

sections.title: Webpack and Turbopack
sections.body: Customize webpack via the `webpack(config, context)` function in next.config. The `context` includes `buildId`, `dev`, `isServer`, `defaultLoaders`, and `webpack` instance. Common uses: adding SVG loaders, custom plugins, alias resolution. Turbopack is configured via the `turbopack` key with `rules` (loader mapping) and `resolveAlias`. Enable Turbopack with `next dev --turbopack` for faster development builds.
